coredata.dc:title: Injectable Chitosan/Gelatin-based microparticles generated by microfluidic systems for synergic chemo-photodynamic therapy against breast cancer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zaker, Mohammad Amin
Ostovar, Shima
Bazargan, Vahid
Salehi, Zeinab
Akrami, Mohammad
Marengo, Marco
coredata.dc:description: Breast cancer is a prevalent cancer impacting women worldwide. Injectable microparticles in cancer therapy are designed to release anticancer medication over several months, delivering therapeutic agents directly to cancer tumors and reducing the need for frequent administration. The application of microparticles as synergistic cancer therapy combines chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy, leveraging the strengths of both treatments. Chitosan (CS) and Gelatin (Gel) as a biological copolymer introduced as a carrier of photodynamic agents (Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and zinc oxide NPs) and a chemotherapy agent (Doxorubicin (DOX)). pH-sensitive CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX microparticles fabricated using microfluidic chips, and the process parameters affected MPs’ size have been discussed. The average size of monodispersed CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX spherical MPs is 35.23 ± 3.80 μm. CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX encapsulated 88.32 % of initial DOX and released 36.2 % of it over 7 weeks in acidic media in a sustainable manner. Evaluation of the photodynamic efficiency of NPs on MCF-7 cells by MTT assay suggested 37.8 % of cells died after incubation under UV light for 10 min. ROS generation of GQDs/ZnO NPs increased by 42.5 % compared control group. Flow cytometry analysis of the 49-day release media from CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs indicated 44.5 % of cells underwent apoptosis. The CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs could serve as a suitable alternative to DOX multiple doses for treating breast cancer within the synergic effect of photodynamic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Photodynamic therapy
Chemotherapy
Microfluidics
Doxorubicin

MPs' size controlling by flows’ parameters in the microfluidic chip

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microscopic pictures of the Flow-Focusing microfluidic chip at the flowrate ratio (QD/QC) of (A) 1/8, (B) 2/8, and (C) 3/8, Change of droplet size in (D) Different flowrate ratio, (E) Different surfactant concentrations.
Microscopic picture and Size distribution of (A) droplets, (B) MPs, FE-SEM images of (C) CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs, (D) ZnO NPs, and (E) TEM image of ZnO NPs.

(A) Chemical structure of Gel, CS, GA crosslinked CS-Gel, and (B) The UV–Vis spectrum of ZnO NPs and GQDs, (C) Chemical interactions of drug, NPs and polymeric network, (D) FTIR spectra of pure material and MPs, (E) The XRD pattern of MPs and pure materials.
(A) MPs' Degradation, (B) DOX cumulative release from CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs at various pH (Results are expressed as mean values ± standard deviation, based on three replicates per group.).

Viability of cell of MCF-7 cells (A) in the dark environment, (B) under UV irradiation, (C) Schematic of the anticancer mechanism of GQDs/ZnO NPs and DOX (D) Flow cytometry of apoptosis and necroes of cells treated with (a) Control, and 49-days media release of (b) CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs, and (c) CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs (E) ROS generation evaluation of cells treated with (a) Control, and 49-days media release of (b) CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs and (c) CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs.
DLS, Zeta potential, Band gap energy, and maximum wavelength of NPs.
Comparison of DOX-loaded delivery systems to the current study.
Parameters of kinetic models for DOX release in different pH.

head.ce:title: Injectable Chitosan/Gelatin-based microparticles generated by microfluidic systems for synergic chemo-photodynamic therapy against breast cancer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, University of Pavia, Pavia, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Breast cancer is a prevalent cancer impacting women worldwide. Injectable microparticles in cancer therapy are designed to release anticancer medication over several months, delivering therapeutic agents directly to cancer tumors and reducing the need for frequent administration. The application of microparticles as synergistic cancer therapy combines chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy, leveraging the strengths of both treatments. Chitosan (CS) and Gelatin (Gel) as a biological copolymer introduced as a carrier of photodynamic agents (Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and zinc oxide NPs) and a chemotherapy agent (Doxorubicin (DOX)). pH-sensitive CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX microparticles fabricated using microfluidic chips, and the process parameters affected MPs’ size have been discussed. The average size of monodispersed CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX spherical MPs is 35.23 ± 3.80 μm. CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX encapsulated 88.32 % of initial DOX and released 36.2 % of it over 7 weeks in acidic media in a sustainable manner. Evaluation of the photodynamic efficiency of NPs on MCF-7 cells by MTT assay suggested 37.8 % of cells died after incubation under UV light for 10 min. ROS generation of GQDs/ZnO NPs increased by 42.5 % compared control group. Flow cytometry analysis of the 49-day release media from CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs indicated 44.5 % of cells underwent apoptosis. The CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs could serve as a suitable alternative to DOX multiple doses for treating breast cancer within the synergic effect of photodynamic therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is a highly common and severe cancer affecting women globally. Various treatments including chemotherapy, endocrine therapy, and surgery often lead to significant side effects such as fatigue, nausea, hair loss, and a heightened risk of infections [1]. Local injectable therapy which injects drugs directly into the tumor site, offers several advantages, such as minimizing systemic side effects, enhanced efficacy, improved patient compliance, and its potential for combination therapy. From 1991 to 2021, the FDA approved over 20 drugs for breast cancer treatment. For example, FDA approved GnRHa (leuprolide acetate and triptorelin pamoate for injectable MPs) for treating premenopausal breast cancer [2].

Doxorubicin (DOX) is widely recognized as a highly effective chemotherapy drug for breast cancer, known for its strong antiproliferative and cytotoxic effects on both primary and metastatic tumors. Its primary mechanisms include intercalating of DNA and producing free radicals such as reactive oxygen species (ROS). Despite its effectiveness, DOX's side effects can significantly impact treatment outcomes and limit its use. Consequently, numerous studies have aimed to enhance DOX's therapeutic efficacy by improving its delivery specifically to tumors [3,4].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a type of anti-cancer treatment that utilizes nanoparticles (NPs) and photosensitizers which when activated by a suitable light source, this photosensitizer generate ROS that cause irreversible damage to the cells [5,6]. PDT is particularly effective for treating skin cancer and tumors near the skin surface, such as breast cancer [7]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and zinc oxide NPs (ZnO NPs) have shown promise as a platform for PDT due to their unique optical properties, making them ideal for generating ROS upon light irradiation. Future research focused on optimizing the PDT performance of GQDs/ZnO NPs and improving targeting strategies will aid in their transition from laboratory research to clinical cancer therapy [8].

Synergistic cancer therapy has significant applications in cancer treatment. The combination of PDT and chemotherapy leverages the strengths of both therapies, chemotherapy uses chemical materials to kill rapidly dividing cancer cells, while PDT employs light-activated photosensitizers to generate ROS [9]. When used together, these therapies can synergically target cancer cells through multiple mechanisms, potentially reducing the likelihood of resistance and improving patient outcomes [10]. Recently, Ishizuka et al. [11] investigate the use of indocyanine green (ICG)-modified liposomes encapsulating paclitaxel (PTX) for combined PDT and chemotherapy in breast cancer treatment. The ICG-PTX liposome is designed to accumulate specifically in tumor tissues due to the properties of the liposomes. Upon exposure to light, the ICG generates ROS, while the PTX is locally released, enhancing the antitumor effects. The ICG-PTX liposomes need frequent admiration due to their relatively fast release. In the following, more studies are needed to investigate combination therapy for breast cancer treatment applications with long-term effects.

Various delivery systems, such as hydrogels, and MPs, can be designed to enhance tumor retention and extend local drug release [12]. MPs are more stable in biological environments than NPs, which helps preserve the drug's integrity and effectiveness [13]. Additionally, MPs can carry larger drug loads, making them suitable for delivering higher doses. They can be injected directly into the cancerous site to deliver therapeutic agents [14]. Once injected, the MPs gradually degrade, releasing the drug over an extended period. This method not only improves the efficacy of the treatment but also reduces the frequency of administration, making it a convenient option for patients. Ongoing research aims to optimize these systems to enhance their targeting capabilities and therapeutic outcomes [15]. Maher et al. [2] successfully fabricated titanium-based MPs encapsulated DOX and apoptosis-inducing ligands to treat bone cancers. They introduced these MPs as a micro-implant which demonstrated significant anticancer effects against bone cancer cells.

Chitosan (CS), a biodegradable polysaccharide, is widely used in cancer drug delivery systems due to its positive charge and mucoadhesive natural [16]. Pang et al. [17] injected 131I-labeled CS–collagen MPs into carcinoma rats significantly extending the survival of the treated rat. In addition, Gelatin (Gel) has certain adhesive, biocompatible, and biodegradable properties for drug delivery applications [15].

Traditional MP synthesis methods, such as emulsion, solvent evaporation, and spray drying typically result in considerable batch-to-batch variability and polydispersity, leading to particles that vary widely in size and shape, which can compromise the consistency and predictability of drug delivery. In contrast, microfluidic systems offer precise control over reaction conditions, allowing for the production of uniform particles with a narrow size distribution [18]. Microfluidic systems can be easily scaled up by integrating multiple microchannels, making them highly suitable for large-scale production [19]. Microfluidic systems provide superior control, efficiency, and scalability compared to conventional microparticle synthesis methods [20]. In this research, CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs are fabricated by using microfluidic techniques. The MPs' size was manipulated by the microfluidic parameters. The incorporation of DOX as an anticancer drug and ZnO NPs and GQDs as photosensitizers into biocompatible injectable CS-Gel MPs holds significant promise for the synergic effect of PDT and chemotherapy for cancer treatment.

2′,7′- Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFDA, 50 μM), CS (low molecular weight), Citric acid, GA, Zinc acetate dihydrate, and Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) were purchased from Sigma Co. DOX obtained from Sobhan Oncology Co., Iran. MCF-7 cell line was sourced from the Pasteur Institute, Iran. Gel was purchased from Quelab Co., Canada.

In our previous work [21], we designed and fabricated a PDMS microfluidic chip. The chip, designed in SOLIDWORKS, had two inlets and one outlet with main microchannels 200 μm deep and 250 μm wide. We 3D-printed the mold using a Phrozen Sonic mini-8k printer, then exposed it to UV for 2 h. PDMS was removed from the mold, and 1 mm punches created outlet and inlet holes. PDMS was exposed to oxygen plasma for 1 min to cross-link, then stored for one week to reverse its intrinsic hydrophobicity.

By heating 10 g of citric acid at 200 °C for 5 min, the substance melts into a yellow liquid. After 30 min, the liquid turns orange, signifying the formation of GQDs. This transformation occurs due to the carbonization of citric acid, where the citric acid molecules decompose and reorganize into a new structure [22].

To begin, 3.46 g of zinc acetate dihydrate (15.8 mmol) was dissolved in 8 mL of methanol and heated to 60 °C. In a separate container, 1.25 g of NaOH was dissolved (31.25 mmol) in 15 mL of methanol. The NaOH solution was then gradually added to the zinc acetate dihydrate solution while magnetically stirring (360 rpm). The solutions were stirred for 3 h, followed by centrifugation and washing the sediment with water 3 times [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MPs through droplet-based microfluidic approach
ce:section.ce:para: First, dissolve 0.5 wt% CS and 0.5 wt% Gel in 1 % acetic acid with magnetic stirring for 3 h to prepare the CS-Gel solution. Add GQDs (7 mg mL−1) and stir for 15 min, then add ZnO NPs to achieve final concentrations of 7 mg mL−1. Add DOX to the CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO solution (2 mg mL−1) and stir for 30 min.

Using two syringe pumps (PHD ULTRA™ CP Harvard Syringe Pump), inject the dispersed (CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX) and continuous (mineral oil with 5 wt% Span 80) phases into the microfluidic chip. Cross-link the CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs with glutaraldehyde (GA) to enhance mechanical strength, stability, and controlled release. Collect the droplets in a crosslinking bath containing GA (2 wt%) and span 80 (5 wt%) of mineral oil solution for 24 h. Wash the MPs with n-hexane three times, then with ethanol via centrifugation at 1500 rpm for 5 min. Freeze-dry the MPs for future analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MPs' size controlling by flows’ parameters in the microfluidic chip
ce:section.ce:para: Varying the flow rates of the continuous (QC) and dispersed phases (QD), and adjusting the Span 80 concentration, changes droplet sizes. QD ranged from 1 to 12 μL min−1, while QC was 1, 2, and 3 μL min−1. Modes with QD/QC < 1 were not explored due to lack of droplet formation. The effect of these 32 modes on MPs’ diameter was investigated. Changing Span 80 concentration affects droplet size. For various Span 80 concentrations, QD was fixed at 2 μL min−1, and Span 80 varied from 1 to 5 wt%.

Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopes (FE-SEM) examined the morphology of MPs and NPs. Size measurements of 150 MPs and droplets were done using an inverted optical microscope and ImageJ software. CV% was calculated as the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean size of MPs. Transmission electron microscopy [24] analyzed ZnO NPs’ structure.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra determined the composition of MPs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis provided insights into the macromolecular arrangement, structure, orientation, and dimensions of ordered regions in pure materials and composites.

To measure the amount of encapsulated DOX (loading efficiency (LE%) and encapsulation efficiency (EE%)), 5 mg of dried MPs was added into 2 mL of 1 wt% HCl solution, stirred the mixture, and 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added. The drug was released by dividing the ethyl acetate phase. The DOX concentration was measured by a spectrophotometer at 480 nm.

The following equation outlines LE% and EE%, where Mi is the total mass of DOX in the primary solution before infusing into the microchannels, Mf is the total mass of DOX in the MPs, and WMPs is the total mass of MPs.
(Equation 1)
EE
of
DOX
( % )
=
M f
/
M i
(Equation 2)
LE
of
DOX
( % )
=
M f
/
W MPs

DOX release from MPs was investigated at pH 7.4 (healthy tissue) and pH 6 (cancer tumor) at 37 °C. Acidic solution was prepared with HCl 0.1 M, and PBS simulated pH 7.4. We added 5 mg of drug-loaded MPs to 10 mL of each solution. Samples were taken at intervals up to 49 days, replacing 2 mL with fresh solution each time. DOX concentration was measured using a spectrophotometer at 480 nm.

The release profile for CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs was established under both conditions. The most suitable kinetic models for DOX release were determined using curve fitting, resulting in the highest R2 values.

The MPs (5 mg) were added to acidic (pH = 6) and alkaline media (pH = 7.4) to investigate their degradation rates. MPs samples were incubated with 10 ml of acidic buffer and alkaline media at 37 °C, on a stirrer at 50 rpm, over 8 weeks. Additionally, to simulate an in vivo physiological environment, 4 mL of the incubated acidic or alkaline solution was replaced weekly with 4 mL of fresh media [25]. The MPs were filtered, washed, and dried. The MPs' mass loss rate (D%) was determined using equation (3) [26], Where M0 was characterized as the initial MPs' mass and Mt as MPs’ mass at time t;
(Equation 3)
D
( % )
=
M 0
−
M t
M 0
× 100

MCF-7 as a human breast cancer cell line was cultured under humidified conditions at 37 °C containing 5 % CO2, using a medium including 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics. In vitro cytotoxicity was assessed for pure DOX, CS-GE MPs, CS-GE/GQDs MPs, CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs, and CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs on MCF-7 cells using the MTT assay over 48 h. To achieve over 75 % confluency, the MCF-7 cells were incubated for one day at a density of 5 × 103/well cells in a 96-well plate with a medium of 1 % PEN, STR (antibiotics), and 10 % FBS. The MCF-7 cell was then incubated with the samples for 48 h, while control cells didn't receive treatment. Subsequently, 100 μL of MTT reagent was added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. The wells were then washed with PBS, and 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. Ultimately, the samples were gently shaken for 15 min, and cell viability was measured by an ELISA microplate reader at a wavelength of 570 nm. The cell viability (%) was obtained by equation (4).
(Equation 4)
Cell
viability
of
MCF − 7
cell
lines
( % )
=
Absorbance Sample
Absorbance Control
× 100

To investigate the PDT effect of GQDs/ZnO NPs, MCF-7 cell viability was assessed using CS-Gel MPs containing GQDs/ZnO NPs and CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs with DOX via MTT assay under dark and UV conditions for 48 h. Treated cells were exposed to a germicidal UV lamp (0.1 mW cm−2) for 10 min after 6 h of incubation to allow NPs to penetrate cells [27].

MCF-7 cells (3 × 105/well) were plated in six-well plates for apoptosis and ROS generation analysis. 500 μL release media of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs after 49 days in an acidic solution was taken. ROS generation by GQDs/ZnO NPs was studied with external light excitation. After 6 h of incubation with 500 μL release media of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs and CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs in the dark, cells underwent UV exposure for 10 min. ROS formation was monitored via DCFH-DA conversion to fluorescent DCF. Cells were incubated with DCFH-DA (5 mg mL−1) for 30 min at 37 °C in the dark and examined using Cyflogic v.1.2.1 software with the FL-1 bandpass filter on a flow cytometer. Each measurement was derived from the average fluorescence intensity of 104 events [27].

Apoptosis was analyzed via PI/Annexin V-FITC staining. Cells were incubated with 500 μL 49-day release media of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO and CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs for 48 h. After 6 h of incubation, the media was exposed to UV light for 10 min. PI/Annexin V-FITC staining was performed following the manufacturer's instructions (BD Biosciences). Cells were gathered, washed with PBS, and prepared in 500 μL of binding buffer, then stained with 5 μL each of PI and Annexin V-FITC. The mixture was incubated in the dark for 15 min, and apoptotic cells were detected by a flow cytometer (Cytomics™ FC 500, Beckman Coulter) [24].

The synergistic effect of chemotherapy-PDT using CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs was proven through the calculation of the combination index (CI) based on MTT assay outcomes. The value of CI was determined by Talalay and Chou's method, equation (5) [28].
(Equation 5)
CI X
=
D
D X
+
P
P X
Where D and P represent the doses of DOX and GQDs/ZnO NPs in combination therapy that result in x% cell death. DX refers to the DOX dose that independently kills x% of cells, while PX denotes the GQDs/ZnO NPs dose that independently achieves the same effect. Combination therapy with a
CI X
value less than 1 indicates a synergistic effect [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: MPs' size controlling by flows’ parameters in the microfluidic chip
ce:section.ce:para: The QC and QD significantly influence droplet size. As shown in Fig. 1 A–C, the spaces between the droplets became progressively smaller as the QD increased, resulting in a higher frequency of droplet formation. In Fig. 1D, it could be observed changes in droplet diameter as flowrates vary. When the QD remains constant, increasing QC leads to a decrease in droplet diameter, as expected due to increased shear stress on the droplet's surface. For instance, by raising the QC from 1 to 12 μL min−1, the average droplet diameter changes from 297.88 to 81.39 μm. Additionally, adjusting the QD while keeping the QC fixed can also impact droplet size.

When Span 80 is introduced to the continuous phase, the gap between the interfacial tension of polymer solution and mineral oil decreases—specifically, increasing the Span 80 concentration from 1 to 5 wt% led to smaller and closer-spaced droplets in the outlet channel. Fig. 1 revealed a decreasing trend in droplet size with increasing surfactant concentration. For instance, at fixed flowrates of QC = 4 and QD = 2 μL min−1, and 1 wt% Span 80, the droplet sizes measured 228.15 μm. The droplet size is at same flowrate with 3 wt% and 5 wt% Span 80 was reduced to 215.11 and 197.57 μm, respectively. To ensure monodispersity and prevent merging, we selected a 5 % Span 80 concentration, which increased the oil phase viscosity and stabilized the droplets [29]. By applying 5 wt% Span 80 as a surfactant, the droplets fabricated with CV and average diameter of 1.25 % and 132.41 μm, respectively. These droplets exhibit high monodispersity, as demonstrated in Fig. 1-C (at QD = 2 and QC = 8 μL min−1).

The average size of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX droplets is 132.41 ± 1.66 μm. The CV is 1.25 % which indicates that the droplets are monodispersed with high accuracy (Fig. 2 -A). Previous research has proven that gelation results in a reduction in droplet size [30,31]. The most significant decrease in diameter happens during the initial transition to the crosslinking bath. In the early stages of the gelation process, the presence of GA boosts the concentration gradient on two sides of droplets, causing H2O to diffuse out of the droplets while GA diffuses in Ref. [32]. As GA penetrates the droplets, covalent crosslinking bonds form, resulting in the release of water and a denser polymeric network. The average size of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs is 35.23 ± 3.80 μm. The CV of MPs after gelation is 10.1 % which is larger than the CV of droplets. It's mainly because of the uncontrolled gelation process of droplets (Fig. 2-B).

CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs’ surface is without any microporosity which is due to the dense polymeric network of CS-Gel (Fig. 2-C). Additionally, the decreased leakage during gelation enhances encapsulation in MPs [33]. The MPs display a high level of roundness, as observed in the microscopic images.

The ZnO NPs show various sizes in a range of 20 <d < 60 nm (Fig. 2-D). The deposition process gives the nanocrystalline ZnO a sphere shape. The TEM image is also confirmed as spherical and ellipsoidal NPs. Also, the ellipsoidal NPs support the hypothesis that variation density of NPs [34]. The zeta potential and mean size of ZnO NPs using DLS/zeta analysis measured 43.1 nm and −25.8 mV, respectively. The average size of GQDs is measured at 8.4 ± 3.4 nm, and also the zeta potential of GQDs is evaluated at −25 mV (Table 1 ).

The UV–visible spectrum of GQDs displays a distinct absorption band about 232 nm corresponded to the π-π∗ electron transition of C=C bonds (Fig. 3 -B). Additionally, GQDs exhibit a absorption peak about 332 nm, associated to the n-π∗ transition of the C=O bond [35]. The UV–Vis spectrum of ZnO NPs reveals absorption peaks at 364 nm, indicative of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO [36]. The optical band gap was determined by the Tauc equation (Equation (6)),
(Equation 6)
(
α hv
)
a
= C ×
(
hv −
E g
)
Here, (Eg) represents the optical band gap, (hv) denotes the incident energy of photon, (C) is a constant, and (a) takes the value of 2 and 1/2 for indirect band gaps and direct band gaps, respectively. The calculated optical band gap for ZnO NPs is 3.14 eV and for GQDs is 3.08 eV [37,38].

In Gel spectra, a peak at 1743 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching (Amide-I bonds). N-H and C-N peaks occur at 1526 cm−1 and 1251 cm−1 (Fig. 3-C) [36]. In pure CS, peaks at 2864 cm−1 and 1612 cm−1 correspond to CH and carbonyl groups [20]. In CS MPs, N–H stretching shifts from 3440 cm−1 to 3414 cm−1, C=N from 1620 cm−1 to 1601 cm−1, and C–N from 1438 cm−1 to 1401 cm−1. New peaks at 1591 cm−1 indicate imine bond formation from GA crosslinking [37,38]. The peak at 2927 cm−1 is sharper due to CH2 groups in GA.

In CS-Gel MPs, the 1743 cm−1 peak (C=N and C=O) is sharper due to Gel's amide group. The 3433 cm−1 peak indicates interaction between C=O in Gel and N-H/O-H in CS, possibly due to Schiff base formation. GA interaction raises N-H bending and lowers C-O stretching in CS and Gel at 1132 and 1068 cm−1.

In GQDs, peaks at 2855–2925 cm−1 relate to C–H in aromatic rings, with peaks at 1407 and 1747 cm−1. The shift of COO− peaks at 1460 cm−1 indicates ionic desorption between COO− of GQDs and NH3+ of Gel and CS.

Incorporating ZnO NPs into CS-Gel/GQDs MPs reduces peak intensity around 1370 cm−1 and broadens hydroxyl peaks, indicating hydrogen bonding. FTIR analyses show DOX loading efficiency with shifts in hydroxyl and N-H stretching modes [39]. The FTIR spectrum of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX shows peaks at 1737 (C-O), 1624, and 1030 (C-O) cm−1 [40]. DOX's carbonyl and carboxyl groups interact with amine groups of CS and Gel, increasing peaks at 3445 (C-H), 1610 (N-H), and 1385 (C-C) cm−1.

The Gel spectrum showed a broad peak at 2θ = 21°, indicating a partly crystalline, amorphous structure [41] (Fig. 3-D). The XRD pattern of CS displays a crystalline peak at 20° (2θ), reflecting its semi-crystalline nature. The crystalline property of CS is linked to its 75 % degree of deacetylation.

The XRD pattern of GQD shows a broad peak at 2θ = 24°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of hexagonal graphite [42]. ZnO NPs show peaks at 2θ = 31.88°, 34.53°, 36.43°, 47.63°, 56.83°, 62.98°, 67.98°, and 69.23°, corresponding to various crystal planes of hexagonal geometry. The Scherrer equation was used to calculate the mean size of the NPs.
(Equation 7)
D =
K λ
β
cos
θ

The mean size of ZnO NPs from the XRD pattern was 41 nm [43]. The crystallinity of CS and Gel declines compared to the pure form (at 21.9°) due to GA cross-linking, which disrupts intra-molecular hydrogen bonds [44]. Adding GQDs to CS-Gel results in the same peaks with a slight shift to a higher angle (23.2°) due to electrostatic attractions and hydrogen bonds [45,46].

Incorporating ZnO NPs into CS-Gel/GQDs MPs enhances peak intensity, indicating ZnO NPs incorporation. Sharp and low-density peaks at 36.4° and a wide peak at 45° are seen in the XRD spectra of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs [47]. Loading DOX into CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs broadens XRD peaks near 2θ = 20°, resulting in a more amorphous structure.

It can be seen from Fig. 4 -A, the weight of the MPs gradually reduced over time. Regarding degradation of MPs in alkaline media, during the initial 3 weeks, the weight of the MPs remained relatively stable, with a weight loss rate of approximately 3.97 %. After three weeks, the degradation rate increased. After six weeks, MPs began to degrade at a significant rate. At the end of 8 weeks, the MPs lost 13.88 % of their weight. The degradation rate of the MPs in acidic media was about 5.81 % within 3 weeks, indicating that the degradation of MPs in acidic conditions is much faster than that in alkaline conditions. At the end of 8 weeks, MPs lost 27.86 % of their weight in acidic media. A part of this weight loss can be related to the release of GQDs and ZnO NPs among the hydrogel network.

MPs' degradation rate varies with pH. Acid hydrolysis is the main mechanism, influenced by oxidative and reductive reactions [48]. Protonation of glycosidic oxygen and CS's amino group occurs in acidic media [49].

Gel behaves similarly across pH conditions. Acidic pH weakens Gel bonds, causing fragmentation and surface erosion, while Gel-based MPs remain stable in alkaline environments [50]. CS/Gel-based MPs degrade faster in tumor environments with enzymes like matrix metalloproteinases and proteolytic enzymes [23,51].

The DOX loading efficiency in CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs was analyzed, and the result showed that the MPs contained 0.1236 mg of DOX per 1 mg of MPs. In other word, the loading efficiency (LE%) of DOX was calculated 12.36 ± 1.19 %. The EE% of DOX in MPs was determined 88.32 ± 2.38 %. As shown in Table 2 , the EE% of DOX in CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs is relatively higher than in other carriers, due to the dense polymeric network and a small amount of DOX lost during the synthesis process.

In acidic conditions (tumor environment), the DOX release profile shows four phases (Fig. 4-B). Initially, rapid drug release occurs within the first two days due to the dissolution and debonding of DOX from MPs' surface. From day 3–6, DOX concentration reaches equilibrium between MPs and solution, with 2 mL medium replaced at each time point. From day 7–21, a moderate release rate is observed due to MPs' swelling and gradual degradation, forming pores. In the final phase (weeks 3–7), DOX is released from macropores and mesopores, with additional pores forming due to further degradation of the CS-Gel matrix. The release curve is described by y% = 1.2585x [48]% + 30.18 %. Notably, the existence of enzymes in an in vivo environment accelerates the degradation of both CS and Gel [49]. In vitro conditions often show a slower drug release rate compared to in vivo conditions [50]. This discrepancy arises due to the lack of physiological factors in vitro that can significantly influence drug release kinetics. The static nature of in vitro studies also fails to mimic the dynamic environment of a living organism, where continuous blood flow and tissue perfusion can enhance drug distribution and release [51]. Therefore, we predict that the drug release rate in vivo will be considerably higher, offering a more accurate representation of the drug's performance in real-world scenarios.

At pH 7.4, the polymeric network of MPs is more packed due to the pH-sensitive behavior of CS. Deprotonation of amino groups in CS increases negatively charged sites, leading to stronger ionic interactions with DOX, impeding its release. In alkanoic conditions, after 49 days, only 8.7 ± 1.5 % of DOX was released, indicating minimal release in healthy tissues and blood flow. This slow release reduces DOX side effects in the patient's body.

The kinetic assessment analyzed release data at two pH conditions (Table 3 ). The best-fitted models were first-order (pH 6) and Korsmeyer-Peppas (pH 7.4). At pH 6, DOX diffuses from the polymeric matrix, while at pH 7.4, both dissolution and diffusion govern release. Korsmeyer-Peppas exponents were 0.1054 (pH 6) and 0.2041 (pH 7.4), indicating fickian diffusion transport. Drug release occurs via molecular diffusion driven by a chemical potential gradient in both environments [62].

The viability of the MCF-7 cell lines was assessed using the MTT assay (Fig. 5 -A). The cells treated with CS-Gel MPs are around 99 % alive after 48 h which approves no toxicity of MPs. The addition of GQDs to CS-Gel MPs increased the toxicity of MPs by 2.5 % [52,53]. In the dark environment, defined as when cells are exposed to natural light, MCF-7 cells show low viability (Fig. 5-A). The toxicity of free DOX is significantly higher than that of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs, where 100 μg ml−1 free DOX kills 87 % of cells, while 100 μg ml−1 CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs only 23 % of cells die. This low toxicity is mainly because approximately just 1 % of encapsulated DOX was released at pH of cell culture media (pH = 6.5–7.5) in 48 h, (based on the release profile of DOX in PBS). In addition, according to the amount of LE% of DOX, 100 μg of MPs contained around 12.3 μg DOX. Consequently, about 1 % of loaded DOX (0.12 μg) is released. The toxicity of MPs in in vivo tumors could be higher than in vitro conditions due to the pH-sensitive behavior of CS-Gel MPs.

The viability of MCF-7 cells was assessed under UV exposure (10 min under a germicidal UV lamp) compared to a dark environment to investigate the PDT effect of ZnO NPs and GQDs (Fig. 5-B). The UV lamp alone showed no significant toxicity, maintaining above 90 % cell viability. However, ZnO NPs/GQDs under UV radiation showed significantly higher toxicity (P < 0.0001) than under natural light. ROS generated by the PDT effect of the NPs can induce oxidative damage, leading to cell death. Cell viability decreased from 92 % to 63 % when exposed to CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs (50 μg mL−1) under UV. Incorporating DOX into the MPs induced a chemotherapeutic effect, resulting in 52.7 % toxicity after 48 h.

ROS production is involved in the action of GQDs/ZnO NPs as photosensitizers in cancer PDT. No ROS was detected without GQDs/ZnO NPs (UV alone). Flow cytometry analysis showed that CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs had 42.5 % more fluorescence intensity than the control group. Loading DOX in MPs led to 15.7 % more ROS generation, as observed by Wang et al. [65].

Fig. 5-E shows annexin V-FITC/PI staining to assess apoptosis in MCF-7 cells that untreated cells maintained high viability, exceeding 98 %. In contrast, treatment with CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs resulted in 9.77 % of cells undergoing early apoptosis. Additionally, 18.4 % of cells were in late apoptosis, and 2.56 % were necrotic following treatment with CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs. In addition, the cells in early stage of apoptosis treated with DOX-loaded MPs were increased (15.2 %). Also, 29.3 % and 5.66 % of the cells were borne late apoptosis and necrosis after treatment with CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs. As could be seen in Fig. 5-C, DOX anticancer mechanism involves DNA intercalation, which leads to more necrotic death rather than GQDs/ZnO NPs [24]. This outcome aligns with the cytotoxicity results combined with UV irradiation (Fig. 5-B), given that MTT analysis investigated cell viability in different doses of MPs, while flow cytometry and ROS generation evaluation investigated 500 μL of 49-day release media. Combining these findings with the ROS generation analysis, it is evident that the increased intracellular ROS levels induced by CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs in cancer cells lead to a significant increase in apoptosis [54].

The synergistic effects of DOX and ZnO NPs/GQDs were investigated by determining the combination index (CI) based on Talalay and Chou's method [28]. The CI30 for UV light-irradiated CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs, at approximately 30 % cell cytotoxicity, was derived from MTT assay results. The cytotoxicity of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs in a dark environment, representing the chemotherapy effect, and CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs alone under UV light irradiation, representing the PDT effect, was investigated at various concentrations. The dose of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs that killed 30 % of cells in the dark (D30) was approximately 2.4 μg mL−1. Additionally, the dose of CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO MPs light-irradiated which killed 30 % of cells (P30) was around 37.1 μg mL−1. The MTT results suggested that the doses of DOX and GQDs/ZnO NPs in CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs in the UV light-triggered chemotherapy-PDT that killed 30 % of cells were 1.2 μg mL−1 (D) and 0.9 (P) μg mL−1, respectively. Consequently, the CI30 value was significantly less than 1, indicating a synergistic effect under UV light in comparison to DOX or GQDs/ZnO NPs alone. Notably, ROS-generating GQDs/ZnO NPs can work synergistically with low doses of DOX. Utilizing CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs for controlled DOX release can prevent severe cytotoxicity in cancer cells without UV irradiation, resulting in high level of targeted phototoxicity.

The CS-Gel copolymer as a biodegradable biomaterial was used to fabricate injectable PDT/chemotherapy-based MPs for intratumoral injection. ZnO NPs and GQDs are utilized as PDT agents while DOX is a chemotherapy agent. The CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs fabricated by droplet-based microfluidic device. The mean size of lyophilized MPs was calculated at 35.23 μm with low CV (10.1 %).

The release profile in acidic media showed about 36.2 % DOX releases in 7 weeks which can be faster in in vivo condition. In alkaline environment, the MPs release just 8.7 % of drug which suggested sustained pH-sensitive drug release profile. The MTT assay within 48 h shows anticancer effect on MCF-7 cell lines which when combined with PDT treatment can kill cancerous cells at an efficient level (52.8 % cell viability). The flow cytometry evaluation of apoptosis and necrose cells confirmed anticancer effect of DOX and PDT agents. The CS-Gel/GQDs/ZnO@DOX MPs resulted in ROS generation which it be shown by flow cytometry evaluation of ROS generation. Taken together, synergistic cancer therapy can be achieved by using controlled release of GQDs/ZnO and DOX against breast without need to frequent admiration.

Mohammad Amin Zaker: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shima Ostovar: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Vahid Bazargan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Zeinab Salehi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Mohammad Akrami: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation. Marco Marengo: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.
